title: Vintage Bar Stool
summary: A simple 2x2-legged bar stool with a slatted seat and a low backrest — straightforward joinery, built on pocket holes.
description:
There's no compound angle or curved leg here — this stool is straight cuts and pocket-hole joints, which makes it a good project if the double-beveled, splayed-leg style of bar stool feels like more than you want to take on right now. The backrest is what gives it its vintage look, built as its own small frame and joined to the seat assembly.

One detail worth not skipping: drilling the apron's pocket holes facing upward, before you attach the legs, means you can screw the seat on from inside the frame later instead of trying to reach up from underneath a finished stool. The stretchers get an extra trick — a pocket hole from two different faces on each 2x2, which locks the joint against racking in both directions instead of just one.

tools.note: For rounding or shaping the backrest top edge, if you want a softer profile than a straight cut.

cutList.note: Extend up to form the backrest posts.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four backrest slats at 8", two backrest sides at 7-1/4" and one backrest top rail at 15" from the Pine. Cut two backrest apron pieces at 15" from the Pine. Cut two front apron at 15", two side aprons at 16", five seat slats at 18" and eight seat slat end caps at 3-1/2" from the Pine. Cut two back legs at 45", two front legs at 29-1/4", two side stretchers at 15" and two front/back stretchers at 16" from the Pine. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to one 8 ft Pine, one 8 ft Pine, two 8 ft Pine and three 8 ft Pine. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the backrest
steps.body: Build the backrest frame from the top rail, sides, and slats, joined with pocket holes and glue. Check it square before moving on — this small assembly sits on top of the back legs and any twist here is obvious at eye level.

steps.title: Build the seat frame
steps.body: Assemble the front, back, and side aprons around the four legs. Drill the aprons' pocket holes facing UPWARD on the inside faces before you attach them — that lets you screw the seat on from inside the frame later, instead of reaching up from underneath a finished stool. If the stool feels like it needs it, add corner braces at this step too.

steps.title: Add the stretchers
steps.body: Attach the stretchers between the legs. Drill a pocket hole into two different faces of each 2x2 stretcher — this locks the joint against racking from two directions instead of one, a trick worth using on any stool with 2x2 stretchers.

steps.title: Add the seat
steps.body: If you drilled the apron pocket holes in the seat-frame step, attach the seat slats from underneath with 1-1/4" pocket screws.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every edge smooth, easing the seat and backrest edges especially — this is a seat someone sits against for a while. Finish as desired.

images.alt: Vintage-style wooden bar stool with a slatted seat and low backrest
